news_summary: Today’s human rights progress highlights global efforts to enhance well-being beyond economic measures, strengthen legal protections for crime victims, improve employment conditions through extended maternity leave, and promote remembrance and safety for work-related accident victims.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés aujourd’hui en matière de droits de l’homme mettent en lumière les efforts déployés à l’échelle mondiale pour améliorer le bien-être au-delà des seuls indicateurs économiques, renforcer la protection juridique des victimes d’actes criminels, améliorer les conditions de travail grâce à l’allongement du congé de maternité, et promouvoir la mémoire et la sécurité des victimes d’accidents du travail.
news_summary_es: Los avances actuales en materia de derechos humanos ponen de relieve los esfuerzos a nivel mundial por mejorar el bienestar más allá de los indicadores económicos, reforzar las garantías jurídicas para las víctimas de delitos, mejorar las condiciones laborales mediante la ampliación del permiso de maternidad y promover la memoria y la seguridad de las víctimas de accidentes laborales.

articles.title: Milestone UN Reports Advocate Moving Beyond GDP
articles.summary:
Click to expand Image
          



  
        
  

        
                      
      
        
              A family collects water lilies from Boeung Tamok lake to sell at the market, Phnom Penh, Cambodia, January 14, 2025.
                    ©  2025 Heng Sinith/AP Photo 
          
    


  
There is growing recognition that current economic metrics fail to capture much of what really matters. Gross domestic product (GDP), which measures total economic activity and is a cornerstone of economic decision-making, is blind to issues such as whether children have quality public schools, people have access to health care, or government institutions are accountable to the public they serve. When economic decision-making is narrowly focused on GDP growth, it leaves human rights behind.But two recent United Nations reports suggest a paradigm shift could be coming. The milestone reports offer concrete recommendations for moving beyond an overemphasis on GDP that could make a major difference for human rights.A report released this month by a UN Secretary General-appointed expert group proposes alternative indicators for measuring economic progress. The dashboard of 31 indicators includes many directly related to human rights, in line with what Human Rights Watch has long advocated, such as key health and education outcomes and the prevalence of discrimination and intimate partner violence. It also includes measures of poverty, inequality, labor rights, environmental sustainability, and public trust.While it is up to governments and international institutions now to adopt and integrate these indicators, they offer a chance to meaningfully shift economic incentives. The report also recommends development of a headline indicator that aggregates multiple dimensions. GDP weighs heavily on everything from credit ratings to borrowing costs, even though it is an unreliable predictor of economic vulnerability and resilience. An indicator that gives weight to human rights, inequality, and environmental sustainability could improve the quality of economic data while incentivizing governments to make decisions that lead to more equitable economies.Another report, a “roadmap” for moving beyond an approach that depends on growth in order to improve respect for economic, social, and cultural rights, was launched on April 21 and comes from the UN special rapporteur on extreme poverty and human rights, Olivier de Schutter. It lists evidence-based policies that the paper argues could better fulfill rights, including adequate funding for public services and universal social security and upholding labor rights, as well the adoption of indicators beyond GDP.These reports do more than build momentum. They show the UN can meet the moment and use its position to try and shift something as globally entrenched as GDP fixation. It is now up to governments to follow suit.
articles.content:
Click to expand Image A family collects water lilies from Boeung Tamok lake to sell at the market, Phnom Penh, Cambodia, January 14, 2025. © 2025 Heng Sinith/AP Photo

There is growing recognition that current economic metrics fail to capture much of what really matters. Gross domestic product (GDP), which measures total economic activity and is a cornerstone of economic decision-making, is blind to issues such as whether children have quality public schools, people have access to health care, or government institutions are accountable to the public they serve. When economic decision-making is narrowly focused on GDP growth, it leaves human rights behind.

But two recent United Nations reports suggest a paradigm shift could be coming. The milestone reports offer concrete recommendations for moving beyond an overemphasis on GDP that could make a major difference for human rights.

A report released this month by a UN Secretary General-appointed expert group proposes alternative indicators for measuring economic progress. The dashboard of 31 indicators includes many directly related to human rights, in line with what Human Rights Watch has long advocated, such as key health and education outcomes and the prevalence of discrimination and intimate partner violence. It also includes measures of poverty, inequality, labor rights, environmental sustainability, and public trust.

While it is up to governments and international institutions now to adopt and integrate these indicators, they offer a chance to meaningfully shift economic incentives. The report also recommends development of a headline indicator that aggregates multiple dimensions. GDP weighs heavily on everything from credit ratings to borrowing costs, even though it is an unreliable predictor of economic vulnerability and resilience. An indicator that gives weight to human rights, inequality, and environmental sustainability could improve the quality of economic data while incentivizing governments to make decisions that lead to more equitable economies.

Another report, a “roadmap” for moving beyond an approach that depends on growth in order to improve respect for economic, social, and cultural rights, was launched on April 21 and comes from the UN special rapporteur on extreme poverty and human rights, Olivier de Schutter. It lists evidence-based policies that the paper argues could better fulfill rights, including adequate funding for public services and universal social security and upholding labor rights, as well the adoption of indicators beyond GDP.

These reports do more than build momentum. They show the UN can meet the moment and use its position to try and shift something as globally entrenched as GDP fixation. It is now up to governments to follow suit.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on significant UN reports proposing a paradigm shift away from GDP-focused economic metrics toward broader indicators that include human rights, inequality, and environmental sustainability. This shift has the potential for broad and meaningful impact on global economic decision-making, promoting more equitable and just societies. The article provides detailed context about the reports, their recommendations, and the potential benefits, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial real-world significance.
articles.personality_title: UN reports propose new ways to measure economic progress beyond GDP
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For a long time, countries have used a number called Gross Domestic Product (GDP) to measure how well their economies are doing. GDP adds up all the goods and services a country produces. But GDP does not show if people have good schools, health care, or fair treatment by the government.

**What happened** – In April 2025, two important United Nations (UN) reports suggested new ways to measure economic progress. One report, made by experts chosen by the UN Secretary-General, introduced 31 indicators. These include health, education, poverty, inequality, workers’ rights, the environment, and public trust. Another report from a UN expert on poverty suggested policies to improve social services, social security, and workers’ rights. Both reports say it is time to use more than just GDP to decide how well a country is doing.

**Impact** – This is important because GDP alone can miss big problems like poverty or unfairness. The new indicators could help governments make better decisions that improve people’s lives. For example, a country might look not only at money earned but also at how well children learn or how safe the environment is. This could lead to fairer and healthier communities.

**What’s next step** – Now, it is up to governments and international organizations to start using these new measures. They will need to create a main indicator that combines many parts like human rights and environmental health. If they do, it could change how countries plan their economies and help build societies that care about more than just money.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s new reports encourage countries to measure success with broader indicators that include human rights and the environment, moving beyond just GDP numbers.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les rapports de l’ONU proposent de nouvelles mesures économiques au-delà du PIB
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Depuis longtemps, les pays utilisent un chiffre appelé Produit Intérieur Brut (PIB) pour mesurer la santé de leur économie. Le PIB additionne tous les biens et services produits. Mais il ne montre pas si les enfants ont de bonnes écoles, si les gens ont accès aux soins ou si le gouvernement est juste.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En avril 2025, deux rapports importants des Nations Unies (ONU) ont proposé de nouvelles façons de mesurer le progrès économique. Un rapport, réalisé par des experts choisis par le Secrétaire général de l’ONU, présente 31 indicateurs. Ils incluent la santé, l’éducation, la pauvreté, les inégalités, les droits des travailleurs, l’environnement et la confiance publique. Un autre rapport d’un expert de l’ONU sur la pauvreté suggère des politiques pour améliorer les services publics, la sécurité sociale et les droits du travail. Les deux rapports disent qu’il est temps d’aller au-delà du PIB.

**Impact** – C’est important parce que le PIB seul peut cacher de gros problèmes comme la pauvreté ou l’injustice. Ces nouveaux indicateurs peuvent aider les gouvernements à mieux décider pour améliorer la vie des gens. Par exemple, un pays pourrait regarder non seulement l’argent gagné, mais aussi la qualité de l’éducation ou la santé de l’environnement. Cela peut créer des communautés plus justes et plus saines.

**Prochaine étape** – Maintenant, les gouvernements et organisations internationales doivent commencer à utiliser ces nouvelles mesures. Ils devront créer un indicateur principal qui rassemble plusieurs aspects comme les droits humains et la santé environnementale. Si c’est fait, cela peut changer la façon dont les pays planifient leur économie.

**Une phrase clé** – Les nouveaux rapports de l’ONU encouragent les pays à mesurer le succès avec des indicateurs plus larges, incluant les droits humains et l’environnement, au-delà du seul PIB.
articles.personality_title_es: Informes de la ONU proponen nuevas formas de medir el progreso económico más allá del PIB
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante mucho tiempo, los países han usado un número llamado Producto Interno Bruto (PIB) para medir qué tan bien está su economía. El PIB suma todos los bienes y servicios que un país produce. Pero el PIB no muestra si los niños tienen buenas escuelas, si las personas tienen acceso a atención médica o si el gobierno es justo.

**Qué pasó** – En abril de 2025, dos informes importantes de las Naciones Unidas (ONU) propusieron nuevas formas de medir el progreso económico. Un informe, hecho por expertos designados por el Secretario General de la ONU, presentó 31 indicadores. Estos incluyen salud, educación, pobreza, desigualdad, derechos laborales, medio ambiente y confianza pública. Otro informe de un experto de la ONU en pobreza sugirió políticas para mejorar los servicios sociales, la seguridad social y los derechos laborales. Ambos informes dicen que es hora de usar más que solo el PIB para decidir qué tan bien le va a un país.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque el PIB solo puede ocultar problemas grandes como la pobreza o la injusticia. Los nuevos indicadores pueden ayudar a los gobiernos a tomar mejores decisiones para mejorar la vida de las personas. Por ejemplo, un país podría fijarse no solo en el dinero ganado, sino también en qué tan bien aprenden los niños o cómo está el medio ambiente. Esto podría llevar a comunidades más justas y saludables.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora, depende de los gobiernos y organizaciones internacionales comenzar a usar estas nuevas medidas. Tendrán que crear un indicador principal que combine varias partes como los derechos humanos y la salud ambiental. Si lo hacen, podría cambiar cómo los países planean sus economías.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los nuevos informes de la ONU animan a los países a medir el éxito con indicadores más amplios que incluyan derechos humanos y el medio ambiente, y no solo el PIB.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a serene Cambodian lakeside scene at dawn, showing a silhouetted family in a small wooden boat gently gathering blooming water lilies, with a subtle, abstract overlay of interconnected icons representing health, education, equality scales, and environmental leaves floating above the water, symbolizing a harmonious shift from economic growth to human rights and sustainability.

articles.title: Chile’s maternity leave expansion led to a three-year employment boost
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM In October 2011, Chile extended postnatal leave from 12 to 24 weeks for women contributing to the country’s social security system. The reform also introduced five days of paid paternity leave. A study published this year by economist Francisca Rojas-Ampuero tracks what happened to women’s employment in the fourteen […]
The post Chile’s maternity leave expansion led to a three-year employment boost first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

In October 2011, Chile extended postnatal leave from 12 to 24 weeks for women contributing to the country’s social security system. The reform also introduced five days of paid paternity leave. A study published this year by economist Francisca Rojas-Ampuero tracks what happened to women’s employment in the fourteen years since.

Formal employment among eligible mothers rose by approximately 15 to 16 percent in the first three years after leave ended. The effect faded between years four and seven, not because it reversed, but because ineligible mothers caught up.

Who was eligible, and who wasn’t

The reform’s cutoff date created a useful comparison group. Mothers of children born on or after July 25, 2011, qualified for the full 84-day extension. Those whose children were born before May 2, 2011, were not eligible. Mothers born between those dates received a partial extension.

Rojas-Ampuero used this variation to estimate the reform’s effects: a regression discontinuity design for leave usage patterns, and a difference-in-differences model to track labor market outcomes over seven years.

Maternity leave substituted for other forms of absence

The sick leave data is telling. Before the reform, many Chilean mothers extended their time at home by claiming sick-child leave, mental health leave, or leave for pregnancy-related illness. After the reform, eligible mothers significantly reduced their use of these alternatives. Extended maternity leave replaced a patchwork of workarounds with a single, legitimate entitlement.

That means the pre-reform system wasn’t preventing extended absences. It was just making mothers work harder to arrange them. The reform simplified the path without meaningfully increasing total time away from work.

The effects were largest for the most vulnerable

Rojas-Ampuero found no significant differences in outcomes by marital status, age at birth, education level, or pre-birth wages. The variable that mattered most was tenure. Women with less than ten months of formal employment in the year before maternity leave gained more than those with stable work histories.

The effect was strongest in municipalities with limited childcare access. For mothers with thin labor market footing and few affordable care options, extended leave helped them stay in formal employment rather than leaving it.

A different result than high-income countries tend to produce

Maternity leave research in wealthy countries tends to find modest or null effects on long-term employment. Rojas-Ampuero’s results diverge, and the explanation is structural: limited childcare availability, weaker job protection, and higher labor market informality create different incentives for mothers deciding whether to return to formal work.

The Chilean reform was designed with those conditions in mind. Policymakers built it explicitly to address low maternal employment among lower-income mothers and compensate for thin childcare infrastructure. The data suggests it worked, at least for the three years where the evidence is clearest.

One caveat worth noting: the study only covers women already in the formal sector before childbirth, a group that’s better positioned than most to begin with. Whether the same effects would show up among informal or lower-income workers isn’t yet known.

Source study: Journal of Development Economics— Who benefits from a maternity leave extension? Evidence from Chile

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on Chile's maternity leave reform that significantly increased formal employment among eligible mothers for three years, demonstrating a positive real-world impact on women's labor rights and economic participation. The reform also introduced paid paternity leave, promoting gender equality. The study provides detailed evidence of the policy's effectiveness, especially for vulnerable women, with broad societal implications beyond a niche group.
articles.personality_title: Chile’s longer maternity leave boosts mothers’ jobs for three years
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In 2011, Chile changed its maternity leave law to help working mothers. The government doubled postnatal leave from 12 to 24 weeks for women paying into social security. It also added five days of paid paternity leave for fathers.

**What happened** – A study by economist Francisca Rojas-Ampuero looked at how this change affected mothers’ jobs over 14 years. It found that formal employment for eligible mothers rose by about 15 to 16 percent in the first three years after their leave ended. The study compared mothers who qualified for the full extension with those who did not, using detailed data and statistical methods.

**Impact** – The longer maternity leave helped mothers stay in formal jobs, especially those with less stable work before having a baby. It also replaced other types of sick or mental health leave mothers used before, making the system simpler and fairer. The biggest benefits were seen in places with fewer childcare options, showing the law helped vulnerable women keep working.

**What’s next step** – Researchers note the study focuses on women already working formally before childbirth. Future work could explore if similar benefits happen for informal or lower-income workers who are less protected by law. Policymakers might also consider how to improve childcare access alongside leave policies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Extending maternity leave in Chile helped many mothers keep their jobs for at least three years, especially those with weaker job security and fewer childcare options.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’allongement du congé maternité au Chili augmente l’emploi des mères pendant trois ans
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En 2011, le Chili a modifié sa loi sur le congé maternité pour aider les mères salariées. Le gouvernement a doublé la durée du congé postnatal, passant de 12 à 24 semaines pour les femmes cotisant à la sécurité sociale. Il a aussi ajouté cinq jours de congé paternité payé.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une étude menée par l’économiste Francisca Rojas-Ampuero a analysé l’impact de ce changement sur l’emploi des mères pendant 14 ans. Elle a montré que l’emploi formel des mères éligibles a augmenté d’environ 15 à 16 % durant les trois premières années après la fin de leur congé. L’étude a comparé les mères ayant droit à l’extension complète à celles qui n’y avaient pas droit, avec des données précises.

**Impact** – Le congé maternité prolongé a aidé les mères à conserver leur emploi formel, surtout celles ayant un emploi moins stable avant la naissance. Il a aussi remplacé d’autres types de congés maladie ou santé mentale utilisés auparavant, simplifiant le système. Les plus grands bénéfices ont été observés dans les zones avec peu d’options de garde d’enfants, montrant que la loi a soutenu les femmes vulnérables.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs soulignent que l’étude concerne les femmes déjà en emploi formel avant la naissance. Il serait utile d’étudier si les mêmes effets apparaissent pour les travailleuses informelles ou à faibles revenus, moins protégées par la loi. Les décideurs pourraient aussi améliorer l’accès à la garde d’enfants en parallèle.

**En une phrase** – L’allongement du congé maternité au Chili a permis à de nombreuses mères de garder leur emploi au moins trois ans, surtout celles avec une sécurité d’emploi faible et peu d’options de garde d’enfants.
articles.personality_title_es: La ampliación del permiso de maternidad en Chile aumenta el empleo de las madres durante tres años
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En 2011, Chile cambió su ley de permiso de maternidad para ayudar a las madres trabajadoras. El gobierno duplicó el permiso postnatal de 12 a 24 semanas para las mujeres que cotizan en la seguridad social. También agregó cinco días de permiso de paternidad pagado.

**Qué pasó** – Un estudio de la economista Francisca Rojas-Ampuero examinó cómo este cambio afectó el empleo de las madres durante 14 años. Encontró que el empleo formal de las madres elegibles aumentó alrededor del 15 a 16 por ciento en los primeros tres años después de terminar su permiso. El estudio comparó a las madres que calificaban para la extensión completa con las que no, usando datos detallados.

**Impacto** – El permiso de maternidad más largo ayudó a las madres a mantener empleos formales, especialmente a aquellas con trabajos menos estables antes de tener un bebé. También reemplazó otros tipos de licencias médicas o por salud mental que usaban antes, haciendo el sistema más simple y justo. Los mayores beneficios se vieron en lugares con pocas opciones de cuidado infantil, mostrando que la ley ayudó a las mujeres más vulnerables.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores señalan que el estudio se centra en mujeres que ya trabajaban formalmente antes del parto. Futuras investigaciones podrían ver si los mismos beneficios ocurren en trabajadoras informales o con menores ingresos, que tienen menos protección legal. Los responsables podrían también mejorar el acceso al cuidado infantil junto con las políticas de permiso.

**Resumen en una frase** – La ampliación del permiso de maternidad en Chile ayudó a muchas madres a conservar sus empleos al menos por tres años, especialmente a quienes tenían menos seguridad laboral y pocas opciones de cuidado infantil.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a mother bird gently feeding her chicks in a sturdy nest perched safely on a strong branch, with a smaller father bird nearby bringing food, set against a soft, natural palette symbolizing extended care and support that helps young life thrive and grow steadily over time.

articles.title: Press release - Victims of crime: improved legal protection and support systems
articles.summary: The European parliament approved new EU rules to reinforce support and protection measures for the victims of crime, as well as their rights in criminal proceedings.Committee on Women’s Rights and Gender EqualityCommittee on Civil Liberties, Justice and Home Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the European Parliament's approval of new EU rules that significantly enhance legal protection and support systems for crime victims across the EU. The reforms include strengthened rights during criminal proceedings, improved access to support services, and special protections for vulnerable groups such as children and victims of sexual violence. The impact is broad, affecting millions of victims across member states, and the article provides detailed context on the measures and their significance.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament approves stronger rights and support for crime victims
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The European Parliament has been working to improve how victims of crime are treated and supported across the European Union (EU). Existing rules were seen as outdated and not strong enough to protect victims’ rights during criminal cases.

**What happened** – On Thursday, Members of the European Parliament (MEPs) voted to approve new laws that update and strengthen protections for victims of crime. The vote was 440 in favor, 49 against, and 84 abstentions. The new rules include better rights for victims during court cases, such as privacy and emotional support, legal help if they cannot afford it, and faster compensation payments. A new EU-wide helpline (116 006) will offer information and support. People can now report crimes online, and special help will be available for vulnerable victims like children and victims of sexual violence, including access to emergency healthcare and abortion where allowed by law.

**Impact** – These changes affect millions of people across the EU who become victims of crime each year. The new laws make sure victims have better protection and support, especially those who are most vulnerable. For example, children will receive age-appropriate care and support in one place, making it easier and less stressful for them. Victims of sexual violence will get quicker access to medical care, which can be critical. The reforms also help people in special situations, like those in immigration centers or care homes, to report crimes safely.

**What’s next step** – The new rules still need to be officially approved by the EU Council. After that, they will be published and all EU countries will have two years to apply these changes in their own laws and systems.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has approved stronger legal protections and support services to better help millions of crime victims across the EU, especially the most vulnerable.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen renforce les droits et le soutien aux victimes de crimes
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le Parlement européen travaille à améliorer la manière dont les victimes de crimes sont protégées et aidées dans l’Union européenne (UE). Les règles actuelles étaient jugées anciennes et insuffisantes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jeudi, les députés européens ont voté pour adopter de nouvelles lois renforçant la protection des victimes. Le vote a été de 440 pour, 49 contre et 84 abstentions. Ces règles offrent plus de droits aux victimes pendant les procès, comme la confidentialité, un soutien émotionnel, une aide juridique si elles n’ont pas les moyens, et un paiement plus rapide des compensations. Un numéro d’appel européen (116 006) sera disponible pour informer et soutenir. Il sera possible de signaler les crimes en ligne, et un soutien spécial sera donné aux enfants et aux victimes de violences sexuelles, avec un accès rapide aux soins médicaux, y compris à l’avortement lorsque la loi nationale le permet.

**Impact** – Ces changements concernent des millions de personnes victimes de crimes chaque année dans l’UE. Les nouvelles règles garantissent une meilleure protection, surtout pour les plus vulnérables. Par exemple, les enfants recevront des soins adaptés à leur âge au même endroit, ce qui les protège du stress. Les victimes de violences sexuelles auront un accès plus rapide aux soins médicaux importants. Les personnes dans des situations particulières, comme les centres d’immigration ou les maisons de soins, pourront aussi signaler les crimes en toute sécurité.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces règles doivent encore être officiellement adoptées par le Conseil de l’UE. Ensuite, elles seront publiées et chaque pays aura deux ans pour les mettre en place.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le Parlement européen a adopté des protections légales et des services de soutien renforcés pour mieux aider des millions de victimes de crimes dans l’UE, en particulier les plus vulnérables.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo aprueba derechos y apoyos más fuertes para víctimas de delitos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El Parlamento Europeo ha estado trabajando para mejorar el trato y apoyo a las víctimas de delitos en la Unión Europea (UE). Las reglas actuales se consideraban antiguas y poco efectivas.

**Qué pasó** – El jueves, los diputados europeos votaron a favor de nuevas leyes que fortalecen la protección de las víctimas. La votación fue 440 a favor, 49 en contra y 84 abstenciones. Las nuevas normas dan más derechos a las víctimas durante los juicios, como privacidad, apoyo emocional, ayuda legal si no pueden pagarla y pagos más rápidos de compensación. Se creará una línea telefónica europea (116 006) para ofrecer información y apoyo. Ahora será posible denunciar delitos en línea, y habrá apoyo especial para niños y víctimas de violencia sexual, incluyendo acceso rápido a atención médica y aborto donde lo permita la ley nacional.

**Impacto** – Estos cambios afectan a millones de personas que sufren delitos cada año en la UE. Las nuevas leyes aseguran mejor protección y apoyo, especialmente para los más vulnerables. Por ejemplo, los niños recibirán atención adecuada a su edad en un solo lugar para reducir el estrés. Las víctimas de violencia sexual tendrán acceso rápido a atención médica importante. También podrán denunciar delitos con seguridad personas en centros de inmigración o residencias.

**Próximo paso** – Las reglas deben ser aprobadas oficialmente por el Consejo de la UE. Después se publicarán y los países tendrán dos años para aplicarlas.

**Resumen en una frase** – El Parlamento Europeo aprobó protecciones legales y servicios de apoyo más fuertes para ayudar mejor a millones de víctimas de delitos en la UE, especialmente a las más vulnerables.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a sturdy, open wooden door symbolizing legal protection, with soft glowing light spilling out onto a calm path lined with gentle hands reaching out in support, alongside a subtle network of interconnected glowing lines representing accessible helplines and digital reporting, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones.

articles.title: Press release - Parliament calls for a European day to commemorate the victims of work-related accidents
articles.summary: On Thursday, Parliament adopted its proposals to reduce the number of work-related accidents, diseases, and deaths in the EU.Committee on Employment and Social Affairs Source : © European Union, 2026 - EP
articles.content:
In remembrance of victims of accidents at work and of occupational diseases, MEPs are asking for 8 August to be designated the European Day in Remembrance of the Victims of Accidents at Work and for the Protection and Dignity of Workers.



The day would aim to raise awareness, they argue, about the importance of prevention and safety at work at public, enterprises, and institutions, together with a roll-out of concrete initiatives in schools and workplaces. The text was endorsed by MEPs with 395 votes in favour, 12 votes against, and 41 abstentions.



Health and safety risks related to AI



Parliament also wants the Commission to assess and address occupational health and safety risks associated with AI and algorithmic management systems. MEPs say that workers engaged through digital labour platforms and those whose tasks, pace and performance are directed or evaluated by AI-based tools, can face heightened risks due to intensified work rhythms and abusive monitoring in algorithmic decision-making.



Heat risks at work



MEPs call on the Commission to assess occupational safety and health risks related to climate-related factors, such as heat stress, extreme weather events, and air pollution. They have asked for better protective and preventive workplace measures to protect workers from extreme heat and climate change impacts, which they want to be recognised as major occupational risk factors.



Inspections



To help achieve safe and healthy workplaces, Parliament underlines the importance of regulatory occupational health and safety inspections and call on EU-countries to strengthen labour inspectorates with permanent staff, adequate resources and institutional independence.



Quote



Chiara Gemma (ECR, IT), rapporteur, said: “We must translate our remembrance of victims of work-related accidents into a concrete commitment to their prevention. Our resolution strengthens support for the ‘Vision Zero’ objective: zero fatalities at work. Safe and dignified working conditions are a fundamental right and a cornerstone of Europe’s social model. Memory must lead to responsibility, awareness and stronger protection for every worker.”



Background



On 8 August 1956, 262 miners lost their life at the tragedy at the Bois Du Cazier mine in Marcinelle, Belgium. The workers were citizens from several countries that are currently EU-countries.



In the EU in 2023, there were 3,298 fatal accidents at work and around 2.8 million non-fatal accidents resulting in at least four days’ absence from work, with serious problems in high-risk sectors such as construction, transport, manufacturing and agriculture.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant policy initiative by the European Parliament to establish a European Day to commemorate victims of work-related accidents and to promote prevention and safety at work. This has broad societal impact across the EU, aiming to reduce workplace accidents and improve workers' health and dignity. It also addresses emerging risks related to AI and climate change, showing a comprehensive approach to occupational safety. The article provides detailed context, including statistics and specific measures, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with tangible benefits for many people.
articles.personality_title: European Parliament proposes day to remember work accident victims and improve safety
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Every year, many workers in the European Union suffer accidents or illnesses caused by their jobs. In 2023, over 3,000 workers died from work accidents, and millions more were injured. This shows the ongoing risks workers face, especially in dangerous sectors like construction and farming.

**What happened** – On Thursday, the European Parliament voted to create a special day on August 8 to remember workers who have died or been hurt at work. This day will help raise awareness about workplace safety and encourage schools, companies, and governments to take action. The Parliament also asked the European Commission to study new risks caused by artificial intelligence (AI) and climate change, like extreme heat, that affect workers’ health and safety. They want stronger inspections and better protection for workers.

**Impact** – This is the first time the EU plans a day dedicated to honoring victims of work accidents while pushing for safer jobs. Recognizing risks from AI and climate change shows a modern approach to protecting workers in today’s changing workplaces. The move supports the goal of zero deaths at work, which could save thousands of lives and improve conditions across Europe.

**What’s next step** – The European Commission will now assess the safety risks related to AI and climate factors and suggest new rules. EU countries are encouraged to strengthen their safety inspections and resources to protect workers better. The new remembrance day on August 8 will be officially celebrated to keep safety in the public eye.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The European Parliament has called for a yearly day to honor workers harmed on the job and pushed for stronger safety measures against new risks like AI and climate change.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Parlement européen propose une journée pour commémorer les victimes d’accidents du travail et améliorer la sécurité
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, de nombreux travailleurs dans l’Union européenne subissent des accidents ou des maladies liées à leur travail. En 2023, plus de 3 000 travailleurs sont morts à cause d’accidents du travail, et des millions ont été blessés. Cela montre les risques importants auxquels les travailleurs sont exposés, surtout dans des secteurs dangereux comme la construction et l’agriculture.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Jeudi, le Parlement européen a voté pour créer une journée spéciale le 8 août afin de se souvenir des travailleurs morts ou blessés au travail. Cette journée vise à sensibiliser à la sécurité au travail et à encourager les écoles, entreprises et gouvernements à agir. Le Parlement a aussi demandé à la Commission européenne d’étudier les nouveaux risques liés à l’intelligence artificielle (IA) et au changement climatique, comme la chaleur extrême, qui affectent la santé et la sécurité des travailleurs. Ils veulent renforcer les contrôles et mieux protéger les travailleurs.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que l’UE prévoit une journée dédiée à honorer les victimes d’accidents du travail tout en poussant pour des emplois plus sûrs. Reconnaître les risques liés à l’IA et au climat montre une approche moderne pour protéger les travailleurs dans les environnements changeants d’aujourd’hui. Cette initiative soutient l’objectif de zéro décès au travail, ce qui pourrait sauver des milliers de vies et améliorer les conditions dans toute l’Europe.

**Prochaine étape** – La Commission européenne va maintenant évaluer les risques liés à l’IA et aux facteurs climatiques et proposer de nouvelles règles. Les pays de l’UE sont encouragés à renforcer leurs inspections de sécurité et leurs ressources pour mieux protéger les travailleurs. La nouvelle journée de commémoration du 8 août sera officiellement célébrée pour maintenir la sécurité au centre de l’attention.

**En une phrase** – Le Parlement européen a demandé une journée annuelle pour honorer les travailleurs victimes d’accidents du travail et a poussé pour des mesures de sécurité renforcées face aux nouveaux risques comme l’IA et le changement climatique.
articles.personality_title_es: El Parlamento Europeo propone un día para recordar a las víctimas de accidentes laborales y mejorar la seguridad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, muchos trabajadores en la Unión Europea sufren accidentes o enfermedades causadas por su trabajo. En 2023, más de 3.000 trabajadores murieron por accidentes laborales y millones más resultaron heridos. Esto muestra los riesgos que enfrentan los trabajadores, especialmente en sectores peligrosos como la construcción y la agricultura.

**Qué pasó** – El jueves, el Parlamento Europeo votó para crear un día especial el 8 de agosto para recordar a los trabajadores que han muerto o sufrido accidentes laborales. Este día ayudará a aumentar la conciencia sobre la seguridad en el trabajo y motivará a escuelas, empresas y gobiernos a tomar medidas. El Parlamento también pidió a la Comisión Europea que estudie los riesgos nuevos relacionados con la inteligencia artificial (IA) y el cambio climático, como el calor extremo, que afectan la salud y seguridad de los trabajadores. Quieren inspecciones más fuertes y mejor protección.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que la UE planea un día dedicado a honrar a las víctimas de accidentes laborales mientras impulsa trabajos más seguros. Reconocer los riesgos de la IA y el clima muestra un enfoque moderno para proteger a los trabajadores en los lugares de trabajo actuales. Esta iniciativa apoya la meta de cero muertes en el trabajo, lo que podría salvar miles de vidas y mejorar las condiciones en Europa.

**Próximo paso** – La Comisión Europea evaluará ahora los riesgos relacionados con la IA y factores climáticos y propondrá nuevas reglas. Se anima a los países de la UE a fortalecer sus inspecciones y recursos para proteger mejor a los trabajadores. El nuevo día de recuerdo se celebrará oficialmente el 8 de agosto para mantener la seguridad en el centro de la atención pública.

**Una frase para recordar** – El Parlamento Europeo ha pedido un día anual para honrar a los trabajadores afectados en el trabajo y ha impulsado medidas de seguridad más fuertes frente a riesgos nuevos como la IA y el cambio climático.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a sturdy, protective helmet resting gently on a workbench bathed in soft, natural light, surrounded by subtle silhouettes of diverse workers symbolized through varied tools and machinery parts, with a warm, glowing background suggesting remembrance, safety, and dignity in the workplace.
